title: Podman Containers not restarting when crashing or when Podman Healthcheck fails
body:
### Issue Description

This is the follow-up to https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28062#issuecomment-3890091791 since that BUG Report was erroneously closed.

As stated in the original Report (if People **actually** read the BUG Report):

> Using my [Supervisor Container](https://github.com/luckylinux/podman-container-supervisor) both Approaches are possible, and **both the Health Check Approach and the internal Loop fail to trigger a restart of the Container 👎**.

Since the original BUG Report addressed the internal Loop (failing after 2 internal Checks), this BUG Report addresses the `HealthCmd` Approach.

As stated previously, also the `HealthCmd` Approach fails to restart the Container.

In one instance I **believed** I found the Culprit: the Generator Parsing might lead to incorrect Results in case some Configuration Settings, which were Properly Commented, are Present !

However **that could not be replicated on a subsequent Run 👎**.

I **believed** it was related to one of the **Commented Entries** in `example-external-trigger-supervisor.container`, but on a 2nd Attempt that didn't make any Difference:
```
[Unit]
Description=Example External Trigger Supervisor Container
#StartLimitIntervalSec=15
#StartLimitBurst=1
#OnFailure=podman-pod-autorestart@example-external-trigger.service

[Service]
#ExecStopPost=/bin/bash -c "systemctl --user restart podman-pod-autorestart@example-external-trigger.service"
#ExecStopPost=/bin/bash -c "/home/podman/podman-tools/podman_pod_autorestart.sh example-external-trigger stop"
```


`example-external-trigger.pod`:
```
[Pod]
# Pod Name
PodName=example-external-trigger

# Network Mode
Network=pasta

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

`example-external-trigger-supervisor.container`:
```
[Unit]
Description=Example External Trigger Supervisor Container
#StartLimitIntervalSec=15
#StartLimitBurst=1
#OnFailure=podman-pod-autorestart@example-external-trigger.service

[Service]
#ExecStopPost=/bin/bash -c "systemctl --user restart podman-pod-autorestart@example-external-trigger.service"
#ExecStopPost=/bin/bash -c "/home/podman/podman-tools/podman_pod_autorestart.sh example-external-trigger stop"
Restart=always

[Container]
ContainerName=example-external-trigger-supervisor

Pod=example-external-trigger.pod
StartWithPod=true

Image=docker.io/library/alpine:latest
Pull=missing

# Simulate Action
# Possible Values:
# - pass
# - fail
#Environment=HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION=pass
Environment=HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION=fail

# Healthcheck Script
Volume=./healthcheck.sh:/healthcheck.sh:ro,z

# Healthcheck Configuration
HealthCmd=["/healthcheck.sh"]
HealthStartPeriod=15s
HealthRetries=2
HealthInterval=10s
HealthOnFailure=kill

# Entrypoint
Exec=/bin/sh -c "while true; do sleep 5; done"

# Entrypoint for Debugging
# Entrypoint=/bin/sh
# PodmanArgs=-it

# Stop Signal (no need to wait 10s for stopping this minimal Container)
StopSignal=SIGKILL
```

`healthcheck.sh`:
```
#!/bin/sh

# Echo
echo "Start Health Check Simulation"

# Print Environment
# printenv

# Abort on Error
set -e

# Be Verbose
# set -x

# Simulate Action
if [ "${HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION}" = "pass" ]
then
    # Do nothing
    x=1

    # Echo
    echo "Health Check OK"
else
    # Echo
    echo "Health Check Failed."

    # Exit with Error
    exit 1
fi

# Exit Normally
exit 0
```

When running **without** anything specified for the Parameter `HealthOnFailure`, nothing happens. The Container stays in an **unhealthy** Status but no Stop or Reboot Action is performed.

When running **with** `HealthOnFailure=kill`, the Container and Pod as killed.
The Pool stays in an **unhealthy** Status but no Stop or Reboot Action is performed.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1.Start the Container and let it crash / fail the Health Check
2.Wait a bit and observe that no reboot action is performed (if `HealthOnFailure=kill`) or nothing at all happens (`HealthOnFailure` not set to anything)


Seems to be quite inconsistent between Runs 👎.

### Describe the results you received

#### With the offending commented Lines

comments.body: You need to decide whether Podman or systemd should be responsible for the restart logic.

What exactly does this Statement mean?

> You need to decide whether Podman or systemd should be responsible for the restart logic.

You said to use `HealthCmd`. This doesn't work most of the Time.

I believe it needs to be `podman`, but again, your Question is not clear.

A 1-Liner Answer is difficult to Decrypt.

Which Configuration Parameter is being questioned exactly ?

If you use `HealthOnFailure=restart`, Podman will restart the container when it reaches an unhealthy state (after two health check failures). I suggest using Podman’s native health checks because they provide the same configurability you’re currently handling through your environment variables (based on your example from the previous issue). Here is a suggested example:

```
[Unit]
Description=Example Supervisor Container with Podman Health Check Restart

[Container]
ContainerName=example-supervisor-healthcheck

# Pod Configuration
Pod=example-app.pod
StartWithPod=true

Image=docker.io/library/alpine:latest
Pull=missing

# Simulate health check behavior
# Set to "pass" to see healthy status
# Set to "fail" to trigger automatic restarts
Environment=HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION=fail

# Healthcheck Script
Volume=./healthcheck.sh:/healthcheck.sh:ro,z

# Healthcheck Configuration
# After 2 consecutive failures, Podman will RESTART the container automatically
HealthCmd=["/healthcheck.sh"]
HealthStartPeriod=15s
HealthRetries=2
HealthInterval=10s
# KEY: Podman handles restart, NOT systemd
# Podman restarts the container in-place without involving systemd
HealthOnFailure=restart

# Main process - simple loop that stays running
Exec=/bin/sh -c "while true; do sleep 5; done"

# Optional: Use SIGKILL for faster shutdown
StopSignal=SIGKILL

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
``` 

If you want to use `Restart=always`, systemd will restart the whole service. This is better for handling container crashes rather than health check failures.

```
[Unit]
Description=Example Supervisor Container with systemd Restart

[Service]
# systemd will restart the service when it exits/fails
Restart=always

[Container]
ContainerName=example-supervisor-systemd

# Pod Configuration
Pod=example-app.pod
StartWithPod=true

Image=docker.io/library/alpine:latest
Pull=missing

# Simulate Action
Environment=HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION=fail

# Main process - runs for 30 seconds then exits to demonstrate systemd restart
# systemd will see the exit and restart the container due to Restart=always
Exec=/bin/sh -c "echo 'Container started at $(date)'; sleep 10; echo 'Exiting after 10s'; exit 1"

# Optional: Use SIGKILL for faster shutdown
StopSignal=SIGKILL

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

I wouldn't recommend mixing these two approaches.

In the other Issue, as you mentioned, I have some `Environment` Variables set, but that is purely because I have a `healthcheck.sh` Script that is doing an internal Loop to avoid killing immediately but rather Wait a bit before starting and allow several Retries. Anyways ...

It might work as you intend/expect, but that defeats the entire Purpose of having a restart System that handles this. It seems though that only half the Failure Cases will be handled: either the Health Check **OR** the Container Crash. **NOT BOTH**. Which to me looks very weird.

Furthermore about your Examples (I just copied-pasted the `HealthCheck` / `Restart=always` if applicable / `Exec` Statements):
1. With `HealthOnFailure=restart` the Container indeed gets restarted. HOWEVER, depend Containers are NOT restarted (which is the whole Point of this `supervisor` Container: to trigger a restart of the entire Pod / sequence of Containers) 😥  
2. This is effectively what I described in the other Issue (https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28062) and does NOT work. Container is not getting restarted and after a while the entire Pod is stopped. Nothing happens after that 😥

I looked into why the container restart isn't working via systemd (due to the crash) or the Podman healthcheck. As the title mentions, it appears that it is actually working as expected, so this is not a bug. Let me provide a further explanation of why issue #28062 isn't behaving the way you want:

When Quadlet generates the container service for a Pod-based container, it automatically adds `BindsTo=example-external-trigger-pod.service`, meaning the container service's lifecycle is tied to the pod service. When `HealthOnFailure=kill` triggers, it kills only the container (not the pod), but the pod subsequently stops because it has no remaining user containers and since the `.pod` quadlet file has no `Restart=` policy in its `[Service]` section, the pod service stays dead, which causes `BindsTo` to actively pull down the container service in a way that suppresses `Restart=always` (systemd doesn't restart services stopped due to dependency resolution). The inconsistency between runs (sometimes one restart, sometimes none) is a race condition between `Restart=always` triggering and the `BindsTo` cascade. The fix is to add `Restart=always` and `RestartSec=5` under a `[Service]` section in your `example-external-trigger.pod` file, so that when the pod stops, systemd restarts it along with the bound container service.

I hope this makes sense. We can close this issue and resolve the discussion.

# Using Podman HealthCmd
With the `HealthCmd` Command the Container is killed but the Pod (and `example-external-trigger-c addy´) stay up:
```
[Unit]
Description=Example External Trigger Supervisor Container

#[Service]
#Restart=always

[Container]
ContainerName=example-external-trigger-supervisor

Pod=example-external-trigger.pod
StartWithPod=true

Image=docker.io/library/alpine:latest
#Image=docker.io/library/debian:trixie-slim
Pull=missing

# Simulate Action
# Possible Values:
# - pass
# - fail
#Environment=HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION=pass
Environment=HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION=fail

# Healthcheck Script
Volume=./healthcheck.sh:/healthcheck.sh:ro,z

# Healthcheck Configuration
HealthCmd=["/healthcheck.sh"]
HealthStartPeriod=15s
HealthRetries=2
HealthInterval=15s
HealthOnFailure=restart

# Entrypoint
#Exec=/bin/sh -c "while true; do sleep 5; done"
Exec=/bin/sh -c "echo 'Container started at $(date)'; sleep 10; echo 'Exiting after 10s'; exit 1"

# Entrypoint for Debugging
# Entrypoint=/bin/sh
# PodmanArgs=-it

# Stop Signal (no need to wait 10s for stopping this minimal Container)
StopSignal=SIGKILL
```

> HealthOnFailure=restart

I think I mentioned that on IRC, that is ultimately the wrong option, you must use stop or kill because the systemd tracks the main pid of the service (conmon) and that one dies with a restart so systemd just stop everything even if podman tries to restart.

The is document https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-create.1.html#health-on-failure-action

So you need at least HealthOnFailure=stop and Service=Restart to make systemd control the restart behavior

@Luap99: when we discussed on IRC, I had it set to `HealthOnFailure=kill` or absent 😉.

I now set `HealthOnFailure=kill` with `Restart=always` and it also gets killed (same as before, without restarting).

I was able to create a working example: 

`example-fixed-caddy.container`
```
Same as yours + caddy file
```

`example-external-trigger-supervisor.container`
```
[Unit]
Description=Example External Trigger Supervisor Container

[Service]
Restart=always

[Container]
ContainerName=example-external-trigger-supervisor

Pod=example-external-trigger.pod
StartWithPod=true

Image=docker.io/library/alpine:latest
Pull=missing

# Simulate failed hc
HealthCmd=/bin/sh -c "exit 1"
HealthStartPeriod=10s
HealthRetries=2
HealthInterval=5s
HealthOnFailure=stop

# Main process
Exec=/bin/sh -c "while true; do sleep 5; done"

StopSignal=SIGKILL
```
> This is a simplified version. I think you can use health checks to handle retries, or a custom script that will exit the container.

`example-external-trigger.pod`
```
[Pod]
# Pod Name
PodName=example-external-trigger

# Network Mode
Network=pasta

[Service]
Restart=always
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
``` 

The Pod service is the problematic part. Podman overwrites the user's `Restart=always` setting to `Restart=on-failure`. I haven't checked the reason why yet, but it seems to be [hardcoded intentionally](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/09faf7844105459a37ec74e8e92aca925456e040/pkg/systemd/quadlet/quadlet.go#L1702C3-L1702C12). 

@ygalblum Can you remember why it is done this way?

I was able to workeround this with:
```
mkdir -p ~/.config/systemd/user/example-external-trigger-pod.service.d
cat > ~/.config/systemd/user/example-external-trigger-pod.service.d/override.conf <<EOF
[Service]
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
EOF
systemctl --user daemon-reload
```
Okay, I will change that thanks.

With `${USER}/podman-tools/podman_pod_autorestart.sh:`:
```
#!/bin/bash

# Determine toolpath if not set already
relativepath="./" # Define relative path to go from this script to the root level of the tool
if [[ ! -v toolpath ]]; then scriptpath=$(cd "$( dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" )" && pwd ); toolpath=$(realpath --canonicalize-missing ${scriptpath}/${relativepath}); fi

# Load functions
source ${toolpath}/functions.sh

# Define user
if [[ ! -v user ]]
then
   user=$(whoami)
fi

# Define Pod Name as the first Input Argument
podname="$1"

# Define Action as the second Input Argument
action="$2"

if [[ "${action}" == "start" ]]
then
    # Echo
    echo -e "Trigger restart of Podman Pod <${podname}>"

    # Echo
    echo -e "Reloading Systemd"

    # Reload Systemd
    systemctl --user daemon-reload

    # Reset all relevant Failed Services
    systemctl --user reset-failed ${podname}-*.service

    # Wait a bit
    sleep 5

    # Restart Pod
    systemctl --user restart ${podname}-pod.service
elif [[ "${action}" == "stop" ]]
then
    # Echo
    echo -e "Stopping auxiliary Systemd Unit podman-pod-autorestart@${podname}.service (no Action performed)"
fi
```

(surely it can be made shorter, but it could also handle extra Logging or some Edge Conditions Check)

The difference, in this Case, is that only **1** Service will be needed, instead of **N** overrides (1 for each Pod).
